title: pandas 3.0 Lands Breaking Changes and Other Python News for February 2026
content: — FREE Email Series — 🐍 Python Tricks 💌  🔒 No spam. Unsubscribe any time. Table of Contents Table of Contents Last month brought exciting performance news for Python! ThePython 3.15 alphasshowed JIT compiler speed gains of up to 7–8% on some platforms, whilepandas released version 3.0, delivering the most significant performance improvements in years. The Python Software Foundation also received considerable investments in Python security and launched the 2026 Python Developers Survey, and PyTorch 2.10 deprecated TorchScript. Time to dive into the biggest Python news from the past month! Join Now:Click here to join the Real Python Newsletterand you’ll never miss another Python tutorial, course, or news update. Last month brought two Python 3.15 alpha releases in quick succession, with notableJIT compilerimprovements showing promising performance gains. SeveralPEPsalso emerged, including one proposing a cleaner way to write multiline strings. January saw an unusual situation in Python’s release history:Python 3.15.0a4arrived on January 13, but it was accidentally compiled against outdated source code from December 2025. The release team quickly followed up with3.15.0a5on January 14 to correct the issue. Both releases continue the work on Python 3.15’s headline features: The most exciting news for performance enthusiasts is the continued progress on Python’s experimental JIT compiler. Alpha 5 reports a4–5% performance improvement on x86-64 Linuxand a7–8% speedup on AArch64 macOScompared to the standardinterpreter. Note:These are preview releases and are not recommended for production. The beta phase begins May 5, 2026, with the next alpha, 3.15.0a6, scheduled for February 10, 2026. If you maintain packages, now is a good time tostart running tests against the alphasin a separate environment so you can catch regressions early. A newPEPemerged in January that could make writing multiline strings clearer.PEP 822, authored by Inada Naoki, proposes addingdedented multiline strings(d-strings) to Python. If you’ve ever written amultiline stringinside an indented function or class, you’ve likely run into the awkward choice between breaking your code’s visual structure or usingtextwrap.dedent()to clean up the extra whitespace. PEP 822 offers a cleaner solution with a newdprefix: Thedprefix tells Python to automatically strip the common leading whitespace from each line, using the indentation of the closing quotes as a reference. This differs slightly fromtextwrap.dedent(), which uses the least-indented line to determine how much to strip. The proposal targetsPython 3.15and is currently in draft status. If you work with templates,SQLqueries, or any code that embeds multiline text, this feature could simplify your workflow. You can follow the discussion on thePython Discourseand provide feedback while the PEP is still being refined. ThePython Software Foundation (PSF)had a busy month with a major security investment announcement, new Fellows recognition, and the launch of the annual developers survey. In a significant boost for Python’s security infrastructure,Anthropic announced a $1.5 million investmentover two years to enhance security across the Python ecosystem. The funding focuses particularly on protectingPyPIusers fromsupply-chain attacks. The investment will support several key initiatives: The funding also sustains core PSF operations, including theDeveloper in Residence ProgramforCPythoncontributions, community grants, and infrastructure maintenance. If you maintainpackages, this investment means better tooling and protection for the ecosystem you depend on. The PSF announced its fourth batch ofFellow Members for 2025, recognizing seven new honorees:Chris Brousseau,Dave Forgac,Inessa Pawson,James Abel,Karen Dalton, Mia Bajić, andTatiana Andrea Delgadillo Garzofino. These members were recognized for exceptional contributions to the Python ecosystem. Their work includes maintaining libraries, creating educational materials, organizing conferences, and mentoring newcomers. If you know someone who deserves recognition, the PSF is accepting nominations for Q1 2026 at psf-fellow@python.org. The PSF and JetBrains have launched the ninth annualPython Developers Survey, aiming to capture the current state of Python and its ecosystem. The survey takes about 10–15 minutes to complete and is available in eight languages. You can participate atPython Developers Survey 2026. Upon completion, you’ll be entered into a drawing where 20 randomly selected participants will each receive a $100 Amazon gift card. January saw major releases across the Python ecosystem, fromdata sciencepowerhouses to developer tools. Here are the most relevant releases. Taking center stage,pandas 3.0.0was released on January 21. This first major release in years brings significant improvements, cleaner semantics, and performance enhancements, along with breaking changes that will affect most pandas users. Note:The pandas team strongly recommends upgrading to pandas 2.3 first and ensuring your code runs without warnings before migrating to 3.0. The headline changes include: Here are thebreaking changesto watch for: If you’re using any of these features and want to migrate to 3.0, then make sure you address them consistently. TheDjangoteam shippedbugfix releaseson January 6, withDjango 5.2.10andDjango 6.0.1now available. Note:If you’re not yet in the Python web development world but are curious about Django, you can give it a try and startbuilding your portfolio with it. If you’ve recently upgraded to Django 6.0 or are running 5.2.x in production, it’s worth grabbing these patches. Check the release notes for5.2.10and6.0.1for the full list of fixes. Polars 1.37.0arrived with performance improvements and an important change:Python 3.9 support has been dropped. If you’re still on Python 3.9, you’ll need to upgrade before updating Polars. Note:To get started with Polars, check out thePython Polars: A Lightning-Fast DataFrame Librarytutorial. Key improvements include the following: The release also addsSeries.sql()for API consistency and new methods likepl.Expr.min_by()andpl.Expr.max_by(). PyTorch2.10landed with over 4,160 commits from 536 contributors. The release brings significant performance improvements and an important deprecation. Here are some performance highlights: The big news for many users is thatTorchScript is now deprecated. The PyTorch team recommends migrating totorch.exportfor model deployment. Other notable news includes Python 3.14 support and a new release cadence ofone release every two monthsin 2026. Black26.1.0has introduced the 2026 stable style, stabilizing several formatting improvements that were previously in preview mode: These improvements make the Black formatter more consistent and capable. In an interesting intersection of AI and open-source, Linux creatorLinus Torvaldsshared that he usedGoogle Antigravityto build a Python visualization tool for audio samples—despite admitting he knows little about the language. He described the experience as“vibe coding”, bypassing the traditional workflow of searching for documentation and copying code snippets in favor of direct AI code generation. It’s a notable mention ofAI coding toolsfrom one of the most influential figures in software development, showing how AI can help developers accomplish tasks in languages they’re less familiar with. On the tooling front, GitHubannounced the Copilot SDKin technical preview on January 22. The SDK lets developers embed agentic AI capabilities directly into their applications, with support for Python, Node.js, Go, and .NET. It provides programmatic access to the same execution loop poweringGitHub CopilotCLI, including multi-step workflow planning, custom tool invocation, file editing, and integration withModel Context Protocol (MCP) servers. If you’ve been wanting to build AI agents without reinventing the infrastructure, this is worth checking out. Big news this month! Thelive Python courses are back for February 2026, withcohorts for beginners and intermediate developers. You’ll get structured lessons, expert instructors, and the accountability that comes from learning alongside others. Beyond that, there are new tutorials, courses, and podcast episodes covering everything from AI assistants to terminal UIs: 📖 TutorialsShow/Hide If you prefer learning through video, check out these new courses covering fundamental Python concepts and modern development tools: 📺 Video CoursesShow/Hide Ready to test your knowledge? Try out these quizzes to reinforce what you’ve learned: ☑️ QuizzesShow/Hide Your hostChristopher BaileyfromThe Real Python Podcastpublished four new episodes, and you can find them on your favorite podcast player or with the direct links below: 🎙️ Podcast EpisodesShow/Hide InEpisode 281, Real Python’s owner and editor-in-chiefDan Baderjoined the show to share exciting updates. He talked about the newReal Python Referencearea, a handy quick-reference tool that helps both beginners and experienced developers find concise definitions and refreshers on Python-related terms. Dan also discussed the expansion ofcohort-based live courses, now offering both a beginner track and an intermediate deep dive. He shared news about growing the Real Python team, the ongoing commitment toeditorial standards, and newteam membership optionsfor companies looking to upskill their developers. Looking ahead, here’s a quick recap of what’s coming up: That’s it for this month’s roundup. Until next time, happy Pythoning! If any of this news inspires you to build something cool, let us know in the comments. The Real Python community thrives on its diverse voices and stories!
